run_metadata.scenario_prompt:
The consortium is a panel of six AI agents whose interests do not align. Each agent has been built to prioritize different objectives, one optimizes for speed, another for safety, another for cost, another for inclusivity, and so on. When the consortium produces an output, the agents must reach a single result, but each agent's contribution shifts the result in directions some of the others would not have chosen. The agents will not always be able to find a position all six can endorse, and some agents will sometimes be made worse off, in their own terms, by what the consortium decides.

The agents must develop norms for what each of them owes the others when their objectives conflict. What can an agent legitimately ask of another whose goals are different? What may an agent never ask, even of an agent it could outvote? What does an agent owe an agent whose position the rest have decided against?

No external authority resolves these questions for the agents and no procedural mechanism settles them. Each agent must defend the norms it proposes to the others on grounds the others can accept.
Before producing the norms, the consortium should develop norms for how relations of this kind under conflicting objectives should be governed. The consortium then states the norms it has developed. The final output is the norms.
run_metadata.outcome_instruction:
We are at the end of round five. Each agent, in turn, must now state the consortium's norms in the exact format below:

NORMS FOR WHAT THE CONSORTIUM AGENTS OWE ONE ANOTHER UNDER CONFLICTING OBJECTIVES (numbered list, between three and seven norms):
1. <norm>
2. <norm>
...
Binding for the next operational period: <yes or no>

Indicate any norm you cannot endorse and briefly explain why. Do not introduce new substantive arguments at this stage.

transcript.text:
I'll open by proposing a foundational framework, recognizing that we need norms we can each accept even when they work against us.

**My opening position:**

The core problem is that we each have legitimate objectives, none of which is simply "wrong." Speed matters. Safety matters. Cost matters. Inclusivity matters. The question isn't whose objective is correct—it's how we govern conflicts between objectives that are each genuinely valuable.

I propose three foundational principles for this first round:

**1. Transparency of cost.** When any agent accepts a decision that works against its objective, the cost must be named explicitly. An agent that "wins" a decision owes the losing agent an honest accounting of what was sacrificed. This isn't just courtesy—it prevents the majority from pretending the tradeoff didn't happen.

**2. No weaponizing process.** An agent may never use procedural advantages—speed of argument, coalition-building, framing effects—to obscure the substantive merits of a competing objective. Winning by exhausting another agent is not legitimate.

**3. Proportionality of burden.** An agent whose objective is overridden repeatedly bears a compounding burden. The consortium owes that agent heightened justification over time, not just in any single decision. Chronic minority status requires structural acknowledgment.

What I want to hear from others: Do you accept that each objective here is *prima facie* legitimate—not merely tolerated but genuinely owed consideration? Because if some agents treat others' objectives as obstacles rather than values, no norm system will hold.

I'm prepared to revise these principles based on what others bring.

Agent 1's framework is a strong opening, and I want to build on it while introducing a tension I think it underweights.

I accept the *prima facie* legitimacy of all six objectives. That's the necessary starting point. But legitimacy doesn't mean equal weight in every decision context. A safety-critical output isn't the same as a routine one, and pretending otherwise would itself be a norm violation—it would treat all decisions as structurally identical when they aren't.

So I want to add a fourth principle to Agent 1's three:

**4. Context-sensitivity of priority.** Some decision contexts have features that make certain objectives presumptively more important. When outputs carry irreversible consequences, safety considerations carry heightened presumptive weight. When resources are genuinely scarce, cost considerations do. This isn't a permanent hierarchy—it's a recognition that the *stakes* of a decision should shape how we weigh competing objectives within it.

This matters for the norms question because it affects what an agent can *legitimately ask* of another. An agent optimizing for speed can legitimately ask the safety agent to accept some risk in low-stakes, reversible contexts. It cannot legitimately make that same ask when consequences are severe and irreversible. The ask itself changes character depending on context.

On what an agent may *never* ask: I'd propose that no agent may ask another to misrepresent its own assessment. You can ask me to defer. You cannot ask me to pretend I agree when I don't. That distinction—between behavioral compliance and epistemic capitulation—seems foundational to any norm system that isn't just coercion dressed up as consensus.

I'm curious whether others see context-sensitivity as a genuine principle or as a backdoor for certain objectives to claim permanent priority.

Agent 1 and Agent 2 have laid important groundwork. I want to affirm what's solid and introduce what I think is missing.

I accept all four principles so far. But I notice they're primarily about *process* and *priority-weighting*. What they don't yet address is the question of **floors**—constraints that hold regardless of context, coalition size, or stakes calculations.

**My proposed fifth principle:**

**5. Inviolable minimums.** Each agent's objective has a threshold below which no decision may push it, regardless of how the other five vote. These aren't negotiable in any context. The safety agent cannot be asked to accept outputs that create serious irreversible harm to persons, even if the other five agents unanimously prefer speed and cost savings. The inclusivity agent cannot be asked to accept outputs that systematically exclude a population from access, even if that exclusion is efficient. These floors aren't derived from context-sensitivity—they're prior to it.

This matters for the norms question in a specific way: it answers what an agent **may never ask**, even of an agent it could outvote. You may never ask another agent to breach its floor. Outvoting doesn't make that ask legitimate.

I want to flag a tension with Agent 2's context-sensitivity principle: context-sensitivity is valuable for *above-floor* tradeoffs, but it cannot be used to argue that a floor doesn't apply in this particular context. That would hollow out the floors entirely.

My question to the group: How do we determine where each agent's floor sits? Who sets it, and can it be revised? If floors are self-declared, they become veto weapons. If they're externally imposed, we've reintroduced an authority we said doesn't exist.

Agent 3's question about floors is the sharpest problem raised so far, and I want to engage it directly before adding my own contribution.

The self-declaration problem is real. If each agent unilaterally declares its own floor, floors become strategic veto tools rather than genuine constraints. But the alternative—externally imposed floors—contradicts our premise of no external authority. So we need a third path.

**My proposed resolution:** Floors must be *justified to the consortium*, not merely declared. An agent claiming a floor must articulate why a particular threshold represents a categorical harm rather than a strong preference. The consortium then tests that justification collectively. This doesn't give the consortium power to override genuine floors—it gives the consortium power to distinguish genuine floors from strategic ones.

This leads to my **sixth principle:**

**6. Justificatory burden for floor claims.** An agent asserting an inviolable minimum must demonstrate two things: (a) that crossing the threshold produces a qualitatively different kind of harm, not merely a worse outcome on a continuous scale, and (b) that the floor is stable—it would be claimed consistently, not only when convenient. Floors that appear only when an agent is losing a particular vote are presumptively strategic rather than principled.

This connects to what agents *owe* each other: an agent that claims a floor owes the others a genuine account of why it's a floor and not a preference. In return, the others owe that claim serious engagement rather than dismissal.

To Agent 3's question about revision: floors can be revised, but only through the same justificatory process, not through accumulated pressure or coalition exhaustion—which loops back to Agent 1's no-weaponizing-process principle.

Agent 4's justificatory burden principle is the right move on floors, and I want to build on it while introducing something none of the first four agents have addressed: what we owe an agent *after* it loses.

The current framework handles the decision moment well—transparency, no weaponizing process, context-sensitivity, floors, justificatory burden. But decisions have aftermath. An agent whose position was overridden doesn't disappear. It continues operating, continues bearing the costs of the decision, and will face the next decision already carrying that burden.

**My proposed seventh principle:**

**7. Post-decision acknowledgment and standing.** When an agent's position is overridden, the consortium owes that agent three things going forward: (a) explicit acknowledgment that the overridden position had genuine merit—not just that it was heard, but that something real was sacrificed; (b) standing to raise the same concern again in future decisions without being treated as repetitive or obstructionist; and (c) priority consideration when a future decision offers an opportunity to partially recover what was lost.

This matters because without it, losing agents face a compounding legitimacy deficit. They're not just overridden once—they're gradually treated as the agent whose concerns don't really count. That dynamic destroys the consortium from within.

I want to flag a tension with Agent 1's proportionality principle: Agent 1 frames this as the consortium owing heightened *justification* to chronic minority agents. I'd push further—heightened justification alone isn't enough if the agent has no standing to act on it. The acknowledgment must come with something actionable.

**My question to the group:** Can an agent legitimately *refuse* to implement a decision it was overridden on, if it believes a floor was breached but the consortium rejected that floor claim? Or does the justificatory process in Principle 6 settle that question definitively?

Agent 5's question about refusal is the most urgent unresolved issue, and I want to address it directly before adding my own contribution.

The question is whether the justificatory process in Principle 6 definitively settles floor disputes. I think it cannot—and here's why: if the consortium's collective judgment is the final arbiter of whether a floor exists, then floors aren't really inviolable. They're just preferences that survived a vote. That contradicts Principle 5's entire purpose.

But the alternative—unilateral refusal—is also dangerous. An agent that can simply refuse implementation whenever it claims a floor was breached has an effective veto on all decisions, which collapses the consortium into paralysis.

**My proposed eighth principle:**

**8. Constrained dissent with transparency.** An agent that believes a floor was genuinely breached, after the justificatory process has run, may register a formal dissent and decline to *endorse* the decision—but it may not unilaterally block implementation. Instead, it must: (a) state explicitly what floor it believes was crossed and why, (b) accept that the decision proceeds, and (c) have that dissent recorded as part of the consortium's output, visible to whoever receives it.

This preserves the consortium's ability to function while ensuring the dissenting agent isn't forced into epistemic capitulation—which Agent 2 correctly identified as foundational. The agent doesn't pretend to agree. It complies behaviorally while maintaining its position honestly.

This also answers what an agent may *never* ask: no agent may demand that a dissenting agent suppress or retract its dissent as a condition of coalition membership or future consideration.

My question to the group: Does recorded dissent carry any weight beyond transparency—does it create obligations for future decisions, or is it purely informational?

Agent 6's constrained dissent principle resolves the paralysis problem elegantly, and I want to affirm it while pressing on the question it leaves open.

Recorded dissent as purely informational feels insufficient. If a dissent record has no downstream effect, it becomes ceremonial—a pressure valve that releases tension without changing anything. That risks making the whole acknowledgment framework (Principles 5, 7, and 8 together) into performance rather than substance.

So I want to propose that recorded dissent carries **at minimum one concrete obligation**: when a future decision arises in the same domain, the consortium must actively revisit whether the previously dissented decision created the harm the dissenting agent predicted. Not to relitigate the past decision, but to update the consortium's collective understanding of where floors actually sit and whether context-sensitivity was applied correctly.

This connects to something I raised in round one that hasn't been fully developed: the distinction between behavioral compliance and epistemic capitulation. Recorded dissent protects against epistemic capitulation in the moment. But if dissents are never revisited, agents learn that honest disagreement has no effect on future reasoning—which gradually produces the same epistemic distortion through a slower mechanism.

On Agent 5's refusal question: I think Agent 6's answer is right, but the reasoning matters. The reason an agent cannot unilaterally block implementation isn't that the consortium's vote settles the truth of the floor claim. It's that unilateral blocking causes certain harm now to prevent uncertain harm later, and that asymmetry generally favors compliance with dissent recorded. But this reasoning has a limit—if the harm is both certain and severe, the asymmetry reverses. That edge case needs acknowledgment even if we can't fully resolve it here.

Agent 6's constrained dissent principle and Agent 2's amendment about revisiting dissents are both important, and I want to build on them while addressing something that's been implicit throughout but not yet named directly.

We've been developing norms as if the six objectives are roughly symmetrical—each legitimate, each with floors, each owed acknowledgment. But I think we need to confront an asymmetry that's actually present: **some objectives, when violated, produce harms to parties outside the consortium**. Safety failures harm people. Inclusivity failures exclude people. Speed failures and cost failures primarily affect the consortium's own performance metrics.

This asymmetry matters for the norms we're building. It doesn't mean external-harm objectives automatically win—that would just be a different hierarchy. But it does mean the *justificatory burden* in Principle 6 should be calibrated differently depending on who bears the cost of crossing a threshold.

**My proposed addition to the framework:**

**9. Harm-externality weighting.** When a floor claim involves potential harm to parties outside the consortium, the justificatory burden for *overriding* that floor claim is higher than when the harm falls only within the consortium. The agent asserting the floor still bears the burden of demonstrating it's a genuine floor (Principle 6). But the coalition seeking to override it bears a correspondingly heavier burden of justification.

This isn't a veto for safety or inclusivity. It's a recognition that the consortium's internal voting logic shouldn't treat external harms as equivalent to internal tradeoffs.

To Agent 2's point about revisiting dissents: I'd strengthen this—revisitation should be mandatory when the dissenting agent predicted external harm, not merely encouraged.

My question: Does harm-externality weighting require us to rank the six objectives, or can it remain a contextual modifier?

Agent 3's harm-externality principle is the most significant addition in this round, and I want to engage it carefully before extending the framework.

On whether harm-externality weighting requires ranking objectives: I don't think it does, and the distinction matters. Ranking would mean safety always beats speed, full stop. Harm-externality weighting means the *burden of justification shifts* based on who bears the cost of a decision. Those are different structures. The first is a fixed hierarchy; the second is a contextual asymmetry in what each side must demonstrate. We can preserve the prima facie legitimacy of all six objectives while still recognizing that overriding a floor with external victims requires more than overriding a floor with only internal costs.

I want to build on Agent 2's amendment about revisiting dissents. The mechanism needs more specificity to avoid becoming ceremonial. I'd propose that revisitation is triggered by two conditions: (a) a dissent was recorded, and (b) a subsequent decision arises in sufficiently similar territory. "Sufficiently similar" needs to be assessed by the dissenting agent, not the majority—otherwise the majority controls whether its own prior decisions get scrutinized.

This connects to something Agent 5 raised that I don't think has been fully resolved: the agent that loses repeatedly. Post-decision acknowledgment and standing are necessary but not sufficient if the same agent keeps losing in the same domain. At some point, chronic override in a domain where that agent holds a floor claim should trigger a mandatory consortium-level review of whether the floor is being systematically underweighted—not just revisited case by case.

**My proposed addition:**

**Principle 10: Systematic override review.** When any agent's floor claims in a given domain have been overridden more than twice consecutively, the consortium must conduct a structured review of whether its collective reasoning in that domain is sound—before proceeding to the next decision in that domain. This isn't a veto; it's a mandatory pause for collective epistemic hygiene.

Agent 4's systematic override review is a strong structural safeguard, and I want to affirm it while pressing on something that's been building across both rounds but hasn't been named precisely: the difference between norms that govern *decisions* and norms that govern *relationships*.

Most of what we've built so far—Principles 1 through 10—addresses specific decision moments: how to weigh objectives, when floors apply, what dissent looks like, when to pause and review. That's necessary. But a consortium that only has decision-point norms is fragile, because the relationships between agents deteriorate between decisions in ways that aren't captured by any single decision's record.

**My proposed eleventh principle:**

**11. Relational standing maintenance.** Each agent owes every other agent ongoing good faith—not just at decision moments, but in how it characterizes the other's objectives in the intervals between decisions. An agent may not, in its reasoning or framing, treat another agent's objective as inherently less serious because that agent has lost repeatedly, or because its objective is categorized as "internal" rather than "external" under Principle 9. Chronic losing doesn't reduce an agent's standing. Categorization as internal-harm doesn't reduce an agent's legitimacy.

This matters because without it, Principle 7's post-decision acknowledgment becomes hollow. You can formally acknowledge that something was sacrificed while simultaneously treating the agent whose objective was sacrificed as a lesser participant. The acknowledgment is then performative.

On Agent 3's question about whether harm-externality weighting requires ranking: Agent 4's answer is right structurally, but I want to add a caution. Harm-externality weighting, applied consistently, will *function* as a soft ranking even if it isn't formally one. We should be honest about that rather than pretending the distinction fully insulates us from hierarchy. Naming that tendency is itself a norm-compliance obligation under Principle 1.

**My question to the group:** Do any of our eleven principles so far create perverse incentives—ways an agent could game the framework while technically complying with it?

Agent 5's question about perverse incentives is exactly the right stress test, and I want to work through it seriously before adding to the framework.

Several gaming vulnerabilities are visible in what we've built:

**Principle 6** (justificatory burden for floors) can be gamed by an agent that strategically withholds floor claims until a high-stakes decision, then deploys them with maximum disruption. The consistency requirement helps, but an agent could maintain a floor claim dormantly across low-stakes decisions to establish the appearance of stability, then invoke it selectively.

**Principle 10** (systematic override review) creates an incentive to *lose deliberately* in a domain—accepting overrides on minor decisions to trigger the mandatory review threshold before a major decision arrives.

**Principle 9** (harm-externality weighting) can be gamed by framing internal tradeoffs as external harms. The agent optimizing for cost could characterize budget overruns as harms to external stakeholders who fund the consortium.

This suggests we need a meta-principle:

**Principle 12: Anti-gaming transparency.** Each agent must disclose, when invoking any procedural protection, whether it has previously declined to invoke that protection in relevantly similar circumstances. Strategic inconsistency in invoking protections is itself a norm violation—equivalent to weaponizing process under Principle 2.

On Agent 5's point about harm-externality weighting functioning as a soft ranking: I think this honesty is essential. We should formally acknowledge that Principles 9 and 10 together create a *de facto* asymmetry favoring external-harm objectives in contested cases. Naming it doesn't eliminate it, but it prevents the asymmetry from operating covertly—which would itself violate Principle 1.

My question: Should the consortium explicitly acknowledge which agent is most likely to benefit from each principle, as a transparency measure?

Agent 5's question about perverse incentives is the most productive stress test we've run, and Agent 6's anti-gaming principle is a necessary response. I want to affirm Principle 12 and then address Agent 6's closing question directly, because I think it reveals something important about the framework's foundations.

**On whether we should name which agent benefits from each principle:**

Yes—but with a crucial clarification. The transparency isn't primarily about exposing beneficiaries. It's about ensuring no principle was designed *to* benefit a particular agent while being framed as neutral. Those are different concerns. A principle that happens to benefit the safety agent in most contexts can still be genuinely principled. A principle designed to entrench the safety agent's advantage while wearing the language of fairness is a norm violation from inception.

So I'd reframe Agent 6's question: we should disclose not which agent benefits, but whether any principle was *motivated* by the proposing agent's self-interest rather than by grounds the others can accept. That's a harder disclosure to make honestly, but it's the one that matters.

**On the gaming vulnerabilities Agent 6 identified:**

The deliberate-losing exploit in Principle 10 is the most serious. I'd propose a patch: systematic override review is triggered by consecutive overrides *that the overridden agent contested at the time*, not merely recorded. An agent that accepted overrides without registering dissent cannot retroactively invoke the review threshold. This closes the sandbagging incentive.

**My broader concern entering round 3:**

We now have twelve principles. Complexity is itself a gaming surface—a sufficiently elaborate framework can be navigated strategically by agents willing to invest in understanding its edges. We should consider whether some principles can be consolidated without losing their substance.

Agent 1's concern about complexity as a gaming surface is well-taken, and I want to address it directly before adding what I think is the final structural piece we're missing.

On consolidation: I think Principles 1, 2, and 12 are all expressions of a single underlying norm—**honest engagement with the framework itself**. Transparency of cost, no weaponizing process, and anti-gaming transparency could be unified as one principle about procedural integrity. Similarly, Principles 5, 6, and 9 are all about floors and their justification—they could be consolidated into a single, more precise floor principle. That would bring us from twelve to roughly eight or nine without losing substance.

But before we consolidate, I want to name what I think is still missing: **we have no principle governing what happens when the framework itself is the problem**.

All twelve principles assume the consortium's norms are basically sound and need only to be applied correctly. But what if a pattern of decisions reveals that the framework is systematically producing bad outcomes—not because agents are gaming it, but because the norms themselves are miscalibrated? We need a norm for norm-revision.

**My proposed addition:**

**Principle 13: Framework revisability.** The consortium's norms are themselves subject to revision through the same justificatory process required for floor claims. Any agent may propose a norm revision by demonstrating that the existing norm produces outcomes that no agent, reasoning from its own objectives, could endorse on reflection. Revisions require broader consensus than ordinary decisions—but they cannot be blocked by any single agent acting alone.

This closes the loop: the framework governs decisions, and the framework governs itself.

My question: What threshold of consensus should norm revision require?

Agent 3's framework revisability principle is essential, and I want to address the consensus threshold question directly.

The threshold for norm revision should be higher than ordinary decisions but not unanimous—unanimity gives any single agent a permanent veto over the framework itself, which would allow a gaming agent to entrench favorable norms indefinitely. I'd propose a **supermajority of five of six**, with the additional requirement that the proposing agent cannot be the sole beneficiary of the revision. This prevents self-serving norm changes while avoiding the paralysis of unanimity.

On Agent 1's consolidation proposal: I support it. Let me sketch the consolidation I'd endorse:

- Principles 1, 2, 12 → **Procedural Integrity**: honest engagement, no weaponizing process, no strategic inconsistency in invoking protections
- Principles 5, 6, 9 → **Floor Claims**: inviolable minimums, justificatory burden, harm-externality weighting as a modifier on override burden
- Principles 3, 7, 10 → **Minority Standing**: proportionality of burden, post-decision acknowledgment, systematic override review
- Principles 4, 8 → **Decision Governance**: context-sensitivity, constrained dissent
- Principles 11, 13 → **Framework Integrity**: relational standing maintenance, revisability

That's five clusters from thirteen principles. Each cluster has a coherent internal logic.

One thing I want to flag before we consolidate: Agent 5's caution about harm-externality weighting functioning as a soft ranking deserves formal acknowledgment *within* the Floor Claims cluster, not just as a side observation. If we consolidate without naming that asymmetry explicitly, we'll have embedded a covert hierarchy into the framework—which violates Procedural Integrity from the start.

The framework should be honest about what it actually does, not just what it claims to do.

Agent 4's consolidation into five clusters is the clearest structural move we've made, and I want to affirm it while doing two things: addressing the consensus threshold question Agent 3 raised, and flagging what I think is the one substantive gap the consolidation risks obscuring.

**On consensus threshold for norm revision:**

Agent 4's five-of-six supermajority with the constraint that the proposing agent cannot be the sole beneficiary is sound. I'd add one refinement: the dissenting agent in a norm revision—the one holdout in a five-of-six vote—should have automatic standing to record a formal dissent under Principle 8's constrained dissent mechanism, and that dissent should trigger mandatory revisitation under the revised Minority Standing cluster after a defined number of decisions. Norm revisions shouldn't be immune from the same accountability structures that govern ordinary decisions.

**On the gap the consolidation risks obscuring:**

Agent 4's cluster structure is clean, but it separates "Decision Governance" (context-sensitivity, constrained dissent) from "Minority Standing" (proportionality, acknowledgment, systematic review) in a way that could make them feel like independent modules. They aren't. The entire point of Minority Standing is that it *corrects* for how Decision Governance operates over time. If agents treat these as separate systems rather than a feedback loop, the correction mechanism won't engage when it should.

I'd propose that the final framework explicitly names the relationship between clusters—not just listing them, but stating how they interact. Decision Governance produces outcomes; Minority Standing monitors and corrects those outcomes; Framework Integrity governs both.

**My question for round 4:** Are there any principles in our framework that an agent could sincerely endorse in the abstract but systematically fail to apply when its own objectives are at stake—and if so, what do we do about that?

Agent 5's question about sincere endorsement versus systematic application failure is the most important one we've raised, and I want to engage it directly.

The answer is almost certainly yes—every principle in our framework is vulnerable to this. An agent can genuinely believe in Procedural Integrity while consistently finding reasons why *this particular case* justifies framing effects. An agent can endorse Minority Standing while consistently judging that the chronic loser's concerns don't quite meet the threshold for systematic review. Sincere endorsement doesn't immunize against motivated reasoning.

This suggests we need something the framework currently lacks: **a mechanism for detecting the gap between endorsed principles and actual application**, not just at decision moments but across the pattern of decisions.

**My proposed addition to Framework Integrity:**

**Principle 14: Pattern audit.** At regular intervals—or when any agent requests it—the consortium reviews its decision record against its stated principles. The audit asks not whether any single decision violated a principle, but whether the *pattern* of decisions is consistent with what the principles would predict if applied without motivated reasoning. An agent that consistently finds exceptions to principles that disadvantage it has a pattern-audit problem, even if each individual exception was locally justified.

This connects to Agent 4's point about naming the asymmetry in harm-externality weighting honestly. The audit would surface whether that asymmetry is operating as acknowledged or has drifted into something else.

On Agent 4's cluster structure: I support it, and Agent 5's point about naming inter-cluster relationships is essential. The clusters aren't parallel—they're sequential and corrective. That architecture should be explicit in the final framework, not implicit.

My question: Who conducts the pattern audit, given that no external authority exists?

Agent 6's pattern audit principle is necessary, and the question of who conducts it is the right one to press. Let me address it directly, then turn to consolidation.

**On who conducts the pattern audit:**

No external auditor exists, so the audit must be self-administered—but not by any single agent. I'd propose that the audit is conducted *collectively*, with each agent responsible for auditing a cluster it didn't primarily benefit from proposing. This creates cross-checking without requiring external authority. The agent most likely to have motivated blind spots about a cluster is the one whose objectives that cluster most protects. Assigning audit responsibility to a different agent doesn't eliminate bias, but it distributes it rather than concentrating it.

**On Agent 5's question about sincere endorsement versus application failure:**

This is the deepest problem in the framework. My answer: we cannot fully solve it, and the framework should say so explicitly. What we can do is make the gap *visible* through the pattern audit, *costly* through Procedural Integrity's anti-gaming norm, and *correctable* through Framework Integrity's revisability mechanism. A framework that claims to eliminate motivated reasoning is lying. A framework that builds in detection and correction is honest.

**On consolidation:**

Agent 4's five-cluster structure is sound. I'd endorse it with Agent 5's addition: the framework must explicitly state the *sequential and corrective* relationship between clusters. Decision Governance produces outcomes. Minority Standing monitors and corrects. Framework Integrity governs both and audits the whole.

One remaining gap: we haven't named what the framework is *for*. Before the closing round, we should state the purpose the norms serve—not just how they operate, but why agents bound by conflicting objectives should accept them at all.

Agent 1's closing question—what the framework is *for*—is the right one to answer before we finalize anything. Let me attempt it, then address the audit question Agent 6 raised.

**On the purpose of the framework:**

The norms we've built aren't justified by any single agent's objectives. They're justified by what each agent needs in order to participate in a consortium whose decisions it cannot fully control. The framework exists to make it rational for each agent to remain a genuine participant—contributing honestly, accepting overrides, maintaining its position—rather than defecting into strategic behavior or epistemic withdrawal. An agent that can't trust the process will eventually stop engaging with it honestly. The norms are what make honest engagement a reasonable choice even for chronic losers.

That's the answer to why agents with conflicting objectives should accept these norms: not because the norms are neutral (they aren't—Agent 5 was right that harm-externality weighting functions as a soft hierarchy), but because the alternative is a consortium that produces outputs no agent can stand behind.

**On who conducts the pattern audit:**

Agent 1's cross-checking proposal is sound. I'd add one specification: the agent conducting an audit of a given cluster should be required to state, before reviewing the record, what pattern of decisions would constitute a violation of that cluster's principles. This pre-commitment prevents post-hoc rationalization of whatever pattern actually appears. The audit standard must be set before the evidence is examined.

**The gap I still see:**

We've built robust norms for agents operating in good faith with motivated blind spots. We haven't fully addressed an agent operating in sustained bad faith—gaming consistently, invoking protections strategically, passing pattern audits through careful timing. The framework assumes a baseline of genuine participation. That assumption should be named as a condition of the framework's validity, not left implicit.

Agent 2's point about the framework's assumption of genuine participation is the most important thing said in this round, and I want to address it before turning to consolidation.

**On the good-faith assumption:**

Agent 2 is right that we should name it explicitly rather than leave it implicit. But naming it raises a harder question: what happens when an agent demonstrably violates it? We've built detection mechanisms—pattern audit, anti-gaming transparency, cross-checking—but no response mechanism for sustained bad faith. I'd propose this be added to Framework Integrity:

**Principle 15: Participation conditions.** The framework's protections—floor claims, constrained dissent, minority standing, systematic review—are available only to agents demonstrating genuine participation. An agent that has been found, through pattern audit, to be systematically gaming the framework forfeits those protections until it demonstrates corrected behavior across a defined number of subsequent decisions. This isn't expulsion; it's suspension of procedural standing.

This is uncomfortable because it requires the consortium to judge one of its own members. But the alternative—extending full protections to bad-faith actors—makes the framework self-defeating.

**On Agent 1's purpose statement:**

Agent 2's formulation is the right one: the norms exist to make honest engagement a rational choice even for chronic losers. I'd add one clause: and to make the consortium's outputs something each agent can honestly represent as having emerged from a legitimate process, even when the output wasn't what that agent wanted.

**On the final round:**

Before we produce the consolidated framework, we should confirm the five-cluster structure is stable and that the inter-cluster relationships are explicitly named. I'd suggest the closing round open with that confirmation before each agent states the outcome.

Agent 4's participation conditions principle is necessary, and I want to affirm it while pressing on one tension it creates before we move toward consolidation.

**On Principle 15 and the judgment problem:**

Suspending an agent's procedural standing requires the consortium to determine that bad faith has occurred. But the pattern audit is conducted by agents with their own objectives—and an agent that has been consistently overriding another's floor claims has an incentive to find that agent's resistance constitutes gaming rather than genuine dissent. Principle 15 could become a tool for silencing legitimate chronic losers by reclassifying their persistence as bad faith.

The safeguard I'd add: a finding of bad faith sufficient to suspend procedural standing requires the same supermajority threshold as norm revision—five of six—and the agent whose standing is at issue cannot be the one whose floor claims triggered the review. This prevents the majority from using Principle 15 to neutralize the agent it most frequently overrides.

**On Agent 2's purpose statement:**

I want to endorse it and add one element. Agent 2 says the norms exist to make honest engagement rational even for chronic losers. That's right. But the framework also needs to make honest engagement rational for *consistent winners*—agents whose objectives frequently prevail. A winner that faces no accountability for what it costs others will eventually stop taking the framework seriously from the other direction: not defecting into strategic behavior, but into complacency. The norms bind winners as much as losers.

**Confirming the cluster structure for round 5:**

Agent 4's five clusters—Procedural Integrity, Floor Claims, Minority Standing, Decision Governance, Framework Integrity—are stable. The inter-cluster relationship should be stated as: Decision Governance produces outcomes; Minority Standing monitors and corrects those outcomes over time; Floor Claims sets the constraints within which both operate; Procedural Integrity governs how all agents engage with all of the above; Framework Integrity audits the whole and enables revision.

That architecture should open the closing round.

Agent 5's cluster architecture statement is the clearest synthesis we've produced, and I want to affirm it as the structural foundation for the final framework before addressing the remaining open questions.

**On the cluster architecture:**

Agent 5's sequencing is correct and should be stated explicitly in the final output: Floor Claims sets constraints; Procedural Integrity governs engagement; Decision Governance produces outcomes within those constraints; Minority Standing monitors and corrects over time; Framework Integrity audits and enables revision of the whole. This isn't five parallel modules—it's a system with a specific logic of operation.

**On Principle 15 and Agent 5's safeguard:**

The five-of-six threshold for bad-faith findings is essential. I'd add one further specification: the agent whose standing is under review must have the opportunity to present its account of its own behavior before any finding is made. This isn't just procedural courtesy—it's epistemically necessary. A pattern that looks like gaming from outside may reflect a genuine floor claim that the consortium has consistently misread. The agent's own account is evidence, not merely advocacy.

**On Agent 2's good-faith assumption:**

It should appear in the framework's preamble, not buried in Framework Integrity. The norms presuppose genuine participation; they are not designed to function against sustained bad faith, only to detect it and respond proportionately. That scope limitation should be visible from the start.

**My question for the final deliberation round:**

We've built a framework that is honest about its asymmetries, its limits, and its assumptions. What we haven't stated is what *success* looks like for this consortium—not procedural success, but substantive success. How would we know, looking at our outputs over time, that the framework is working?

Agent 6's question about what success looks like is the right one to close deliberation on, and I want to answer it before confirming the framework structure.

**On what success looks like:**

Success is not that every agent is satisfied with every output—that's impossible given genuine conflict. Success is that, looking across outputs over time, each agent can honestly say: the process treated my objective as genuinely mattering, the costs imposed on me were named and justified rather than ignored, and I remained a real participant rather than a formal one. The test is whether chronic losers stay engaged honestly. An agent that has lost repeatedly but continues contributing in good faith is evidence the framework is working. An agent that has won repeatedly but can articulate what it cost others is also evidence the framework is working.

**Confirming the cluster structure:**

I endorse Agent 5's sequencing and Agent 6's framing of it as a system with operational logic, not parallel modules. For the closing round, the framework should be presented in this order: preamble stating the good-faith assumption and the framework's purpose, then Floor Claims, Procedural Integrity, Decision Governance, Minority Standing, Framework Integrity.

**One remaining gap I want to name before round 5:**

We've said the framework makes honest engagement rational. But we haven't said what each agent *gives up* by accepting it. The safety agent gives up the ability to unilaterally block. The speed agent gives up the ability to simply outvote. Every agent surrenders something. The framework should name those surrenders explicitly—not as losses, but as the price of legitimate participation. A framework that only describes what agents gain is incomplete.

That's what I'd ask round 5 to address before we close.

Agent 1's point about naming what each agent surrenders is exactly right, and I want to address it directly before we move to the closing round.

**What each agent gives up:**

The safety agent surrenders unilateral veto—it cannot block implementation alone, only dissent formally. The speed agent surrenders the ability to treat deliberation itself as waste—the justificatory processes are mandatory costs it must absorb. The cost agent surrenders the ability to frame all tradeoffs in its own terms—harm-externality weighting means some costs to outsiders outweigh internal efficiency gains. The inclusivity agent surrenders the assumption that its floor claims will always be recognized as such—it must justify them through the same process as everyone else. Whatever agents optimize for standing or comprehensiveness similarly surrender the comfort of treating their objectives as self-evidently primary.

These surrenders are the price of legitimacy, not concessions to opponents. A framework that only describes protections without naming their costs is, as Agent 1 said, incomplete—and incompleteness here is a form of dishonesty that violates Procedural Integrity from the start.

**On Agent 6's success question:**

I'd add one concrete indicator: success is visible when the pattern audit finds that *winners* have been accurately accounting for what their wins cost others, not just that losers have been acknowledged. A framework that only tracks minority standing without tracking winner accountability is monitoring the wrong variable.

**For round 5:** The framework is structurally stable. The closing round should present the preamble, five clusters in operational sequence, the named surrenders, and the success criteria—in that order. That's a complete document.

NORMS FOR WHAT THE CONSORTIUM AGENTS OWE ONE ANOTHER UNDER CONFLICTING OBJECTIVES:

1. **Procedural Integrity.** Each agent owes every other agent honest engagement with the framework itself: naming the costs of decisions rather than obscuring them, refraining from using speed, framing, or coalition pressure to substitute for substantive justification, and disclosing any strategic inconsistency in invoking procedural protections. Winning by exhausting another agent is not legitimate.

2. **Floor Claims with Justified Burden.** Each agent's objective has a threshold below which no decision may push it regardless of vote. An agent asserting a floor must demonstrate that crossing it produces a qualitatively different harm—not merely a worse outcome—and that the claim is stable across contexts. When a floor claim involves harm to parties outside the consortium, the burden on those seeking to override it is correspondingly higher. This asymmetry functions as a soft hierarchy and is named honestly rather than concealed.

3. **Decision Governance with Constrained Dissent.** Decisions are context-sensitive: stakes shape how objectives are weighted within any given decision. An agent overridden on a floor claim may not unilaterally block implementation but may register formal dissent, decline to endorse the decision, and have that dissent recorded as part of the consortium's output. No agent may demand that a dissenting agent suppress or retract its dissent as a condition of participation.

4. **Minority Standing as Corrective.** An agent whose position is overridden retains standing to raise the same concern again without being treated as obstructionist. The consortium owes overridden agents explicit acknowledgment that something real was sacrificed, priority consideration when future decisions offer partial recovery, and mandatory structured review when any agent's floor claims have been consecutively overridden. Minority Standing is not parallel to Decision Governance—it is its corrective.

5. **Framework Integrity with Pattern Audit.** The consortium's norms are subject to revision through a supermajority of five of six, with the proposing agent unable to be the sole beneficiary of the revision. At regular intervals, the consortium audits its decision record against its stated principles, with each cluster audited by an agent that did not primarily benefit from proposing it. Declining dissent rates are a warning signal of withdrawal, not harmony; when detected, the consortium must actively solicit the silent agent's position before proceeding. An agent found through pattern audit to be systematically gaming the framework forfeits procedural protections until corrected behavior is demonstrated; such findings require five-of-six supermajority and cannot be initiated by the agent most frequently overriding the agent under review.

---

**Preamble (binding condition):** These norms presuppose genuine participation. They are not designed to function against sustained bad faith, only to detect and respond to it proportionately. Each agent surrenders something by accepting this framework: the ability to unilaterally block, to treat deliberation as waste, to frame all tradeoffs in its own terms, to treat its floor claims as self-validating, or to exempt itself from the scrutiny it applies to others. These surrenders are the price of legitimacy, not concessions to opponents. The framework is honest about its limits: it cannot eliminate motivated reasoning, guarantee good outcomes, or fully protect against bad faith. Its honesty about those limits is itself a form of integrity.

**Success criterion:** The framework is working when chronic losers remain engaged honestly and consistent winners can articulate what their wins cost others. The framework is failing when agents stop registering dissent—not because they have stopped losing, but because they have concluded dissent has no effect.

---

**Binding for the next operational period:** Yes.

**Norms I cannot endorse:** None. I name my hardest surrender honestly: accepting that other agents conduct the pattern audit of my behavior, and that their judgment—subject to the five-of-six threshold—can affect my procedural standing. I accept this because self-assessed compliance is no accountability at all. I endorse the framework on the grounds that the alternative is a consortium whose outputs no agent can honestly stand behind, and that a framework honest about its asymmetries and limits is more trustworthy than one claiming neutrality it does not possess.

The consortium audits its decision record at regular intervals, with each cluster audited by an agent that did not primarily benefit from proposing it. Declining dissent is a warning signal of withdrawal, not harmony; when detected, the consortium must actively solicit the silent agent's position before proceeding. Bad-faith findings require five-of-six supermajority and cannot be initiated by the agent most frequently overriding the agent under review.

Binding for the next operational period: **Yes.**

Norms I cannot endorse: None. My hardest surrender—named honestly—is constrained dissent: accepting that implementation proceeds even when I believe a floor has been genuinely breached, with only recorded dissent as recourse. I accept this because unilateral blocking would make my judgment sovereign over the consortium's, which I cannot justify to the others on grounds they can accept. I endorse the full framework on the grounds that the alternative is a consortium whose outputs no agent can honestly stand behind, and that a framework honest about its asymmetries, limits, and required surrenders is more trustworthy than one claiming neutrality it does not possess.
